You are an adversarial production-readiness assessor for the hc-assay engine at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay (dataset-agnostic, data-sovereign empirical-ML/methodology engine; optional backends import-guarded; transcripts committed + scrubbed intentionally). It has survived NINE hardening passes and CI is green — easy findings are gone. Report ONLY genuine, concrete, FALSIFIABLE production-readiness defects with a real failure mode and a file:line/symbol location; no style nits. If a dimension is clean, return few/zero findings — do NOT invent. Read real code (Read/Grep/Bash; "python -m pytest", "source .venv/bin/activate"; gh for CI). Branch harden/pass-10 (from merged main @ pass 9).

YOUR DIMENSION: actual CI execution.
Inspect the REAL CI (gh run list/view --branch main; read ci.yml). Reproduce each lane's EXACT command (bare pytest; no-extras core lane; cold uv resolve; matrix 3.11-3.14). Find: tests importing optional backends without pytest.importorskip that fail the core lane; any gate that passes vacuously or fails nondeterministically; the lockfile --exclude-newer determinism (could a NEW in-range release before the 2026-06-14 cutoff still flip resolution?); coverage floor correctness; the require_green_ci.sh stale-run hole as a CI finding. Confirm main CI is actually green and STAYS green.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

1	name: ci
2	
3	on:
4	  push:
5	    branches: [main]
6	  pull_request:
7	
8	# Least privilege (#H-008): the default GITHUB_TOKEN can be repo-write; this workflow only reads
9	# the code and uploads artifacts, so pin the token to read-only at the top level. Any job needing
10	# more must opt in explicitly with its own `permissions:`.
11	permissions:
12	  contents: read
13	
14	jobs:
15	  # Dependency-free core: the engine must lint, type-check, and test with NO extras installed
16	  # (optional backends are import-guarded; integration tests skip). This guards ADR-0006.
17	  core:
18	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
19	    strategy:
20	      matrix:
21	        python-version: ["3.11", "3.12", "3.13", "3.14"]
22	    steps:
23	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
24	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
25	        with:
26	          python-version: ${{ matrix.python-version }}
27	      - name: Install (no extras, hash-pinned)
28	        run: |
29	          # Install the dev toolchain from the hashed, audited core lockfile so tests run against
30	          # EXACTLY the versions pip-audit scanned and the SBOM describes — not a live PyPI
31	          # resolution where a malicious patch release could execute under test (#F-004). The
32	          # core lock carries only the `dev` extra (no backends), preserving the ADR-0006 guard.
33	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
34	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-core.lock
35	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
36	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
37	      - name: Lint (ruff)
38	        run: ruff check src tests
39	      - name: Format check (ruff)
40	        run: ruff format --check src tests
41	      - name: Type-check (mypy)
42	        run: mypy
43	      - name: Test (pytest)
44	        run: pytest -q
45	
46	  # Extras lane: install the real backends and run the integration suite so the backend code
47	  # paths actually execute, and mypy type-checks against the REAL (not ignored) library types.
48	  # Service-dependent tests (ollama/qdrant/postgres) skip when the service is absent in CI; the
49	  # extra-only tests (langgraph graph, mlflow lifecycle, OTel, numpy primitives, methodology
50	  # e2e) run for real.
51	  integration:
52	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
53	    services:
54	      postgres:
55	        image: postgres:16
56	        env:
57	          POSTGRES_PASSWORD: postgres
58	          POSTGRES_DB: assay
59	        ports: ["5432:5432"]
60	        options: >-
61	          --health-cmd "pg_isready -U postgres"
62	          --health-interval 5s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 10
63	      qdrant:
64	        image: qdrant/qdrant:v1.12.4
65	        ports: ["6333:6333"]
66	    env:
67	      # loopback host keeps the data-sovereignty guard satisfied; creds are redacted on error
68	      ASSAY_TEST_POSTGRES_URL: postgresql://[REDACTED]@localhost:5432/assay
69	    steps:
70	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
71	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
72	        with:
73	          python-version: "3.12"
74	      - name: Install (all extras, hash-pinned)
75	        run: |
76	          # Install from the hashed all-extras lockfile so the integration suite executes against
77	          # exactly the audited, pinned versions — closing the window where a compromised patch
78	          # release within a pyproject range could run during the real-backend tests (#F-004).
79	          python -m pip install --upgrade pip
80	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
81	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
82	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
83	      - name: Type-check against real backend types (mypy)
84	        run: mypy
85	      - name: Verify service containers are reachable (no silent skip)
86	        # #H-009: the live Postgres/Qdrant tests SKIP when the service is unreachable, so a
87	        # misconfigured/never-started container would let the integration job report GREEN without
88	        # actually exercising the backends. Fail loudly here if either service isn't up, so the
89	        # gate cannot pass vacuously.
90	        run: |
91	          set -euo pipefail
92	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
93	            pg_isready -h localhost -p 5432 -U postgres && break || sleep 2
94	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Postgres service never became ready"; exit 1; }
95	          done
96	          for i in $(seq 1 30); do
97	            curl -fsS http://localhost:6333/readyz && break || sleep 2
98	            [ "$i" = "30" ] && { echo "::error::Qdrant service never became ready"; exit 1; }
99	          done
100	          echo "postgres + qdrant reachable"
101	      # Real backends now run: langgraph graph + interrupt/resume, mlflow lifecycle, OTel,
102	      # numpy primitives, the T3 drain/classification (synthetic SDK, no metered call), AND —
103	      # via the service containers — the live Postgres checkpointer + Qdrant vector store.
104	      # Ollama and the metered frontier tier are not in CI and skip honestly.
105	      - name: Integration + unit tests with coverage
106	        # --cov-fail-under ENFORCES a floor (#K-OPS-3): without it `pytest --cov` prints coverage
107	        # but exits 0 at any level, so the coverage "gate" was vacuous — a refactor deleting tests
108	        # or adding a large untested module would still pass green. The floor (90) sits below the
109	        # current ~97% with headroom for environment-dependent skips; ratchet it up over time.
110	        run: pytest -q --cov=assay_engine --cov-report=term-missing --cov-fail-under=90
111	
112	  # Supply-chain: fail on known-vulnerable dependencies (a security/privacy engine must not ship
113	  # with advisories). The scan runs against the committed, fully-pinned, hashed lockfile
114	  # (requirements.lock) so the audit is reproducible across runs and matches what installs
115	  # (#108, #130, #131, #132, #145, #146). An allowlist file (.pip-audit-ignore, one advisory id
116	  # per line, '#' comments and blank lines allowed) covers accepted/unfixable ones.
117	  audit:
118	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
119	    steps:
120	      - uses: actions/checkout@34e114876b0b11c390a56381ad16ebd13914f8d5 # v4
121	      - uses: actions/setup-python@a26af69be951a213d495a4c3e4e4022e16d87065 # v5
122	        with:
123	          python-version: "3.12"
124	      - name: Install pip-audit
125	        # Pin the audit tooling to exact versions (#G-022): an unpinned pip-audit can change
126	        # advisory results / exit behavior between releases, making the supply-chain gate
127	        # non-reproducible (a green commit could fail tomorrow on a tooling bump) — the analogue
128	        # of the uv pin (#F-031).
129	        run: python -m pip install --upgrade pip && python -m pip install pip-audit==2.10.1
130	      - name: Lockfiles are in sync with pyproject (reproducibility guard)
131	        run: |
132	          # Pin uv to the exact version the lockfiles were generated with (#F-031): an unpinned
133	          # `uv` upgrade can change output format/ordering/annotations and produce spurious diffs
134	          # that fail this gate on every PR even when the lockfiles are current.
135	          python -m pip install uv==0.11.21
136	          # Pin the RESOLUTION to a fixed point-in-time index (#K-OPS-1). Without --exclude-newer,
137	          # uv resolves the newest in-range version of every (largely unconstrained, transitive)
138	          # dep at CI wall-clock time, so this "reproducibility" gate goes red the instant ANY
139	          # upstream publishes an in-range release — on PRs that touch no dependency. The date is
140	          # the single source of truth in .uv-exclude-newer (regenerate_lockfiles.sh reads the same
141	          # file), so the regen here matches the committed lock exactly. Bump that file to update.
142	          EXCLUDE_NEWER="$(tr -d '[:space:]' < .uv-exclude-newer)"
143	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --all-extras --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements.regen.lock
144	          # ignore the auto-generated header (timestamp/command line) when diffing
145	          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements.regen.lock) \
146	            || { echo "::error::requirements.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
147	          # the core lockfile (dev extra only) backs the hash-pinned core lane (#F-004) and must
148	          # stay in sync too.
149	          uv pip compile pyproject.toml --extra dev --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock
150	          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-core.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-core.regen.lock) \
151	            || { echo "::error::requirements-core.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
152	          # the build-backend lockfile (hatchling etc.) backs the hash-pinned --no-build-isolation
153	          # editable install (#J-003) and must stay in sync.
154	          printf 'hatchling\neditables\n' > /tmp/build-reqs.in
155	          uv pip compile /tmp/build-reqs.in --universal --generate-hashes --exclude-newer "$EXCLUDE_NEWER" -o /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock
156	          diff <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' requirements-build.lock) <(grep -vE '^[[:space:]]*#' /tmp/requirements-build.regen.lock) \
157	            || { echo "::error::requirements-build.lock is stale — run: scripts/regenerate_lockfiles.sh"; exit 1; }
158	      - name: pip-audit (reproducible known-vulnerability scan)
159	        run: |
160	          set -euo pipefail
161	          # --strict: any dependency that cannot be collected/audited FAILS the gate (no silent
162	          # skips). --require-hashes: every pin must carry a hash. Build --ignore-vuln args as an
163	          # array so allowlist entries can't word-split or glob-expand, and read the file
164	          # newline-agnostically so the last line is honored even without a trailing newline.
165	          IGNORES=()
166	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
167	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
168	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
169	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
170	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
171	          fi
172	          pip-audit --strict --require-hashes --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock "${IGNORES[@]}"
173	      - name: SBOM (CycloneDX) from the lockfile
174	        run: |
175	          set -euo pipefail
176	          # Emit a machine-readable software bill of materials for the pinned dependency set so
177	          # downstream license/transitive monitoring has a stable artifact to track (#147). No
178	          # `|| true` mask (#F-012): a pip-audit failure must fail the step, and the SBOM must be
179	          # non-empty, so a broken/absent SBOM cannot pass CI green.
180	          # Apply the SAME allowlist as the gate (#G-015): pip-audit exits non-zero on ANY finding
181	          # regardless of output format, so without the allowlist an ACCEPTED advisory would fail
182	          # SBOM generation here even though the gate step passed — coupling SBOM emission to vuln
183	          # status and making the allowlist ineffective. The gate step already ran first, so a
184	          # NON-allowlisted advisory has failed the job before we reach here.
185	          IGNORES=()
186	          if [ -f .pip-audit-ignore ]; then
187	            while IFS= read -r id || [ -n "$id" ]; do
188	              case "$id" in ''|\#*) continue ;; esac
189	              IGNORES+=(--ignore-vuln "$id")
190	            done < .pip-audit-ignore
191	          fi
192	          pip-audit --progress-spinner off -r requirements.lock --format cyclonedx-json -o sbom.cyclonedx.json "${IGNORES[@]}"
193	          test -s sbom.cyclonedx.json
194	          echo "SBOM emitted ($(wc -c < sbom.cyclonedx.json) bytes)"
195	      - name: Upload SBOM artifact
196	        if: always()
197	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
198	        with:
199	          name: sbom-cyclonedx
200	          path: sbom.cyclonedx.json
201	          if-no-files-found: error
202	      - name: License gate (deny strong-copyleft in the pinned dependency set)
203	        run: |
204	          set -euo pipefail
205	          # Fail if any dependency carries a strong-copyleft license incompatible with the
206	          # Apache-2.0 distribution (#147). Scan the SAME hash-pinned set pip-audit audited, not a
207	          # live `pip install -e .[all]` resolution that could pull different versions than the
208	          # lockfile pins (#F-013). Advisory monitoring of the rest is handled by Dependabot.
209	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements.lock
210	          pip install --require-hashes -r requirements-build.lock  # hashed build backend (#J-003)
211	          pip install --no-build-isolation --no-deps -e .
212	          python -m pip install pip-licenses==5.5.5  # pinned for reproducible license output (#G-022)
213	          pip-licenses --format=json > licenses.json  # scan the dep set, not system tooling (#J-006)
214	          python scripts/license_gate.py licenses.json
215	      - name: Upload license report
216	        if: always()
217	        uses: actions/upload-artifact@ea165f8d65b6e75b540449e92b4886f43607fa02 # v4
218	        with:
219	          name: license-report
220	          path: licenses.json
221	          if-no-files-found: warn
222	
223	  # Single required status check (#F-014): branch protection SHOULD require ONLY `all-checks`.
224	  # It fans in every other job, so adding a new job automatically gates merges without anyone
225	  # remembering to update the branch-protection rule — and `audit`/`integration` can never be
226	  # silently dropped from the merge gate by requiring just `core`.
227	  #
228	  # CAVEAT (#CI-1): branch protection is UNAVAILABLE on this repo's plan (private + free tier →
229	  # the protection API returns 403), so NOTHING here actually blocks merging a red PR — this job's
230	  # result is computed but not enforced by the platform. That gap let CI go silently red across
231	  # passes. Until the repo is public (out of scope — would publish transcripts) or on a plan with
232	  # protection, the compensating control is `scripts/require_green_ci.sh` (run before every merge)
233	  # + the documented policy in docs/MERGE-POLICY.md. Do NOT --admin-merge past a red `all-checks`.
234	  all-checks:
235	    if: always()
236	    needs: [core, integration, audit]
237	    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
238	    steps:
239	      - name: Verify all required jobs succeeded
240	        run: |
241	          results="${{ needs.core.result }} ${{ needs.integration.result }} ${{ needs.audit.result }}"
242	          echo "job results: $results"
243	          for r in $results; do
244	            if [ "$r" != "success" ]; then
245	              echo "::error::a required job did not succeed ($r)"
246	              exit 1
247	            fi
248	          done
249	          echo "all required jobs succeeded"

===require_green_ci.sh===
#!/usr/bin/env bash
# Pre-merge CI gate (#CI-1) — compensating control for an unenforceable required status check.
#
# Branch protection / required status checks are UNAVAILABLE on this repo's plan: it is private on
# a free tier, so `gh api repos/<owner>/<repo>/branches/main/protection` returns HTTP 403
# ("Upgrade to GitHub Pro or make this repository public"). GitHub therefore does NOT block merging
# a PR whose CI is red — the `all-checks` fan-in job is computed but nothing consumes it as a gate.
# That is the exact mechanism by which CI went silently red across several hardening passes (merged
# via --admin / un-gated). Making the repo public to enable protection is out of scope (it would

#
# Until one of those changes, THIS is the gate: it exits non-zero unless the latest `ci` run for the
# branch concluded success. Run it before every merge and refuse to merge on failure:
#
#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh [branch]      # defaults to the current branch
#   scripts/require_green_ci.sh && gh pr merge <n> --merge --delete-branch
set -euo pipefail

branch="${1:-$(git rev-parse --abbrev-ref HEAD)}"
read -r status conclusion < <(
  gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1 \
    --json status,conclusion -q '.[0].status + " " + (.[0].conclusion // "none")'
)
echo "latest ci run for '$branch': status=$status conclusion=$conclusion"
if [ "$status" = "completed" ] && [ "$conclusion" = "success" ]; then
  echo "CI is green — safe to merge."
  exit 0
fi
echo "::error::CI for '$branch' is not green (status=$status conclusion=$conclusion) — do NOT merge (#CI-1)." >&2
exit 1

findings.title: require_green_ci.sh pre-merge gate never compares the CI run's commit SHA to HEAD — certifies merges for untested commits (stale-run hole)
findings.claim: The compensating pre-merge gate that substitutes for unenforceable branch protection (#CI-1) queries only `gh run list --branch "$branch" --workflow ci.yml --limit 1` and checks that run's status/conclusion. It never reads or compares the run's `headSha` against the local/remote tip it is about to merge. So the gate reports 'CI is green — safe to merge' based on whatever the most-recent run for the branch was, regardless of which commit that run tested.
findings.why_real: Concrete, reproducible failure mode: (1) push commit A, CI goes green; (2) add a NEW commit B (or amend/force-push) on the same branch; (3) run `scripts/require_green_ci.sh` before CI for B has started (or while B's run is still in_progress so the latest *completed* success is still A's). `gh run list --limit 1` returns A's green run, the script prints 'safe to merge' and exits 0, and the operator merges B — code CI never ran on. I confirmed the exact mechanism: `gh run list --branch main --workflow ci.yml` exposes a per-run `headSha`, and right now the local pass-10 HEAD is 17b4eb52 while `require_green_ci.sh main` would certify green off run a49b3f68 — a DIFFERENT commit. This is precisely the un-gated-red-merge class the script was written to prevent (the doc admits CI 'went silently red across several hardening passes'), but the implementation leaves the door open for the stale-/wrong-SHA case. Branch protection that would normally enforce SHA association is unavailable on this plan, so this script is the ONLY gate.
findings.suggested_fix: Fetch the run's `headSha` alongside status/conclusion (`--json status,conclusion,headSha`) and require it to equal the commit being merged: `git rev-parse "$branch"` (or `origin/$branch` after a fetch). Fail closed if the latest completed run's headSha != the tip SHA, with a message like 'latest green CI ran on <sha>, but HEAD is <sha> — push and wait for CI'. Also treat status!=completed as not-green (already done) AND refuse when no run exists for the exact SHA.

findings.title: --exclude-newer lock determinism can still be flipped by an unyanked/re-published pre-cutoff release, turning the audit reproducibility gate red on dependency-free PRs
findings.claim: The audit lane regenerates all three lockfiles with `uv pip compile ... --exclude-newer 2026-06-14T00:00:00Z` and diffs against the committed locks, asserting reproducibility. `--exclude-newer` filters candidates by PyPI upload-time, not by what existed when the lock was generated. A previously-yanked (or deleted-and-reuploaded) distribution whose upload-time is BEFORE the cutoff can become visible to the resolver AFTER the lock was committed; on the next regeneration uv may then pick a different in-range version even though no source/pyproject change occurred.
findings.why_real: I reproduced the gate locally with pinned uv 0.11.21 and the committed cutoff: all three locks are currently in sync (LOCK_IN_SYNC / CORE_LOCK_IN_SYNC / BUILD_LOCK_IN_SYNC), so the gate is sound today. But the determinism guarantee rests on the assumption that the set of pre-cutoff releases is immutable, which PyPI does not strictly guarantee (unyank, delete+reupload of a fixed file). If that happens, the `diff ... || exit 1` step fails on PRs that touch no dependencies, making the supply-chain gate go red for reasons unrelated to the PR — the same class of false-red the --exclude-newer pin was introduced to prevent, just from the other direction. Fail-closed (not a security exposure), hence low severity.
findings.suggested_fix: Make resolution depend on a frozen index snapshot rather than only a time filter: either vendor/pin to a uv resolution lockfile (uv.lock) checked for byte-equality, or cache the resolved candidate set, or relax the audit gate so a pure lockfile-content diff with identical hashes for the SAME versions does not fail (compare resolved version+hash pairs rather than re-resolving). At minimum document the unyank caveat next to the #K-OPS-1 comment so an unexpected red audit on a no-dep PR is diagnosable.
